speaker: interviewee
text: Now, let me back up. Before my sisters came in 1941, when I came to the United States, they put us-- all of us-- in Ellis Island. Of course, everybody went through Ellis Island. And we stayed in Ellis Island for a day and then we went to a place called Pleasantville, New York, where there was a childrens' home. And we stayed there for six weeks. And they were trying to find a place to take all three boys anywhere in the United States. That's the JSSB, which is now Jewish Vocational Service. At that time, it was called to Jewish Social Service Bureau. So the only place in the country they could find was there was a German family in Detroit, Max and Tecla Adler, A-D-L-E-R, that said they would take us on temporary basis. These were Germans that had immigrated, German Jews that had emigrated in '38, didn't have their own children, and said they would take us on a temporary basis.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went all by train from New York to Detroit and we stayed with Max and Tecla Adler, supposedly on a temporary basis. But of course, the United States entered the war and the whole country became involved in fighting a war and there was little effort being made to try and place us in homes. So the Adlers were asked to keep us. Unfortunately, my experiences with the Adlers were not pleasant.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Germans, they were proud to be Germans. And even though they were Jewish, he served in the German Army in World War I and was shot right through the face. And he had war memories of his fatherland. And they were not Nazis, but they were proud Jews. And they had no children and they had strict German ways. And I won't tell you how strict, but it was strict. And Joe, of course, being much more mature at 13 when we came here than his years showed, having experienced his background, couldn't handle that and rebelled against them. And a year later, he-- he said, I'm going to New York, to our family in New York. So of course, on his own. He says, I'm leaving. And so they had to put him in-- in someplace else and he wound up in boarding houses. Martin and I stayed, and we stayed because we were more adaptable than Joe and we didn't put up that much resistance. And we were happy just to have a roof over our head and be able to have great food on the table. So we put up with all this [NON-ENGLISH] craziness of the Germans. I'll just give you one example of craziness. Mrs. Adler loved to have-- loved to eat sweet Land O'Lakes butter. And there was rationing. And we-- in rationing time, you have to have ration books in order to get it. But she wanted to use her rationing for butter only for sweet Land O'Lake's butter. And she sent me on scouring-- scouting trips to look at it. And I knew every grocer, every supermarket, I knew everybody on Dexter Avenue from Joy Road all the way to Davison. And being young and being very-- being very cute, I would always find somebody to part with a quarter pound of sweet lake butter-- sweet lakes-- I mean sweet butter. And I'd always come back triumphant, I got you some butter. And that made me a hero. I mean, if I could do those things-- if I could get her sugar, if I could get her butter, if I could get anything that was on-- that was-- that was restricted, she was happy. So that was her way of rewarding me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk a little about your schooling.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Now, in-- in 1946, we left the Adlers and Martin and I wound up in a boarding house. And it was a house that contained-- had 13 children and it was being operated by JSSB. And we were all fed and we were pretty well left to ourselves. I was able to find jobs. I worked in a drugstore, I worked at a grocery store attendant, I did everything. In 1948, finally they found me an American foster home to take me in-- finally-- that was the Sonny and Eva Stein family. And all my children know about them. And I went to live with them in 1948 and I had a wonderful year with them. In 1949, when I graduated high school, I had very little money saved up. I had worked that summer on a potato chip route as an assistant. When the potato chip company made driver stopped, it was the Evercrisp potato chip, I would help him load and unload the-- the goods. One of the routes he had was at the Maccabee's building right on Woodward and-- Woodward near Wayne university. And my original plan was to go only to night school. That's all I could afford to do. So on his one stop at the Maccabee's building, I said-- I went over to register part time, I told him I was going to register the part time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after registering part time, on the way back I stopped at the student center, which was the housing place. And I said-- I went in there and I said, if I could go to school full time, could I get a job? And they said, well, you can work at the candy counter. But in order for you to work at the candy counter, you need-- need to have a place to stay here, you know. So they, they sent me up to [INAUDIBLE], the head of the housing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went up to the housing department and I said, if I can get a job, I'll be able to-- and get a room to stay-- I'll able to go full time to school. And he says, I'll give you a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was at Wayne University?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wayne University. So I went back and registered full time [LAUGHS] and I walked back and I had a job and I had a part time-- I had housing. So I went back to the Stein family and said, I'm sorry, I love you, but I'm moving into Wayne University. Well, all I had is enough money to register, the $75 it cost to register. I didn't have any money for food. I think I had $1 to last me six weeks. And we weren't getting paid for six weeks. That's the way it was. So I had enough money to pay for tuition, enough money to pay for room-- which was, I think, $5 a week, something like that, $5 a week. And I started out that way, with $1 in my pocket.

speaker: interviewer
text: You eventually went to law school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, we survived. We were able to survive. And from that, of course, I-- I went full time, worked my way through all the way. And going to law school was also by accident. My best friend, Albert Cooper, who was in French Club with me and a philosophy major and we were very close friends, one day I ran into him for coffee and he said he was-- he had been entered law school. I said, it can't be. How can you, a philosophy major, go into law school? He says, well, it is, it's just like playing chess, it's logic. So he started explaining to me contracts and torts, and within that period of time-- 45 minutes to an hour-- that we had coffee, he convinced me that law school was something worthwhile and not just the dusty smutty books that you would see in a library. And I went back and-- to the law school and they said, fill out an application and then forgot about it. A few weeks later, I got an acceptance. I said, oh my God, I'm accepted in law school. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: So now it became a serious business. And that's how I started. When I started, of course, I went to-- to school full time and I had to work my way through. So in order to do that-- in order to do that, I had to take a job as a proctor. A proctor was a night watchman on the floor where I lived. And so I took the midnight to 4:00 shift. That allowed me to do my homework from midnight to 4:00. So what I would do is I would-- I would do my studying from-- until nine o'clock at night, then from 9:00 or 10:00, then sleep a couple of hours, then do my homework for four hours while I was sitting as the proctor and then go back to sleep from 4:00 to 7:00, 4:00 to 6:00.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the split shift, so I wound up with a split shift all through law school until I finally had to transfer to night school. But that-- that's how you survived. And I survived through law school on my own and became a lawyer. And at the age of 26, I was a lawyer. And just did it without being able to ask for anyone for assistance. When you have to, you do things--

speaker: interviewer
text: You later went on to have a family. I know you have four children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell is their names on tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I have to tell you more. I'll tell you that I met my wife, she's-- we're now divorced. I met her on one of my trips to Europe. I met her in Paris at the American Express. And it was a whirlwind courtship and we were married in London, England after six weeks. And I brought her over and her mother, my mother-in-law, so she could be reunited with her friend and get married. Brought them over together in 1960, August 1960. And this marriage lasted 15 years. It was fulfilling for me, brought me four children, wonderful children. After 15 years, my former wife, Giselle, wanted out. She fell in love with somebody else. And it was a very painful experience for me, very painful because I thought I was a good father and a good husband. But she decided that it was time to change her life so she decided to move out. And she left me, thank God, the custody of all four children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: David is the oldest, he's 35 now. He was born September 2, 1961. Debbie is next, she's 32. She was born January 20, 1930-- 1964. Daniel is the third. He's 29. He was born January 9, 1967. And Darren is the youngest. He's 27. He was born April 10, 1969. Those are my four children. They're my jewels, the crown jewels of my family. And fortunately, I have been blessed with my four children. They've achieved beyond my wildest expectations. David followed me-- my footsteps and has become a lawyer. Darren, the youngest, followed me in my footsteps, he's also become a lawyer. Daniel now is following in my footsteps and he's a senior in law school and doing very well-- on the dean's list and expects to be a lawyer next year and would also like to join me. Debbie went her separate way. She became involved in Jewish education and she's now getting a PhD in Jewish education at the University of San Francisco. And she's now working on a thesis. One thing I do want to say-- I mean, one might wonder with all my background, with all my experience why I have not become bitter, why I have not carry a chip on my shoulder. That's an interesting point. I-- probably one of the things that I decided early in my life is that whatever traumatic experiences that I-- that I have had, I would not burden my children with that. And I would want them to have a life of their own, the best that their family could offer and have sheltered them from the pain. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [CRYING]|

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I shouldn't have tears in my eyes, but-- these are tears of joy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGH] They're tears of happiness because whatever I have done, whatever decisions I have made seem to be the right ones. I'm now blessed with four wonderful children that are making their own life, doing very well. I couldn't be making me prouder. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Whatever I have experienced is now part of history. If it-- if there's any benefit to that experience, it perhaps taught me how to do things right-- the right way. And if life is any teacher of what to do right, I have been fortunate in learning early in life what to do right and to set my life perhaps as a beacon for others to follow. I'm glad my children did that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you. Do you want-- do you need--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the earliest family picture showing my father, my mother, my two brothers, and my sister. Reggie was not yet born. My father is the person with the hat, my mother is the woman standing on the right. The one in the middle between my parents is Fannie. The one sitting on the bottom left is Joe. In the middle is Martin and the one on the lower right is me. This picture was taken approximately 1934, when I was about three years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This is a picture of Chateau de la Hille, the family of Chateau de la Hille in southern France. And this picture was taken sometime in 1941, early 1941. And it's a group picture and I am in that picture on the lower right hand corner. I'll put my pen next to me. This is me right here. Next to me is my brother, Martin, right here with his hand on his eye. And my brother, Joe, I'll move my pen right to him now. There is Joe. Can you see him? There he is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was taken in 1941. And somehow, this group picture was taken by somebody at the-- at the castle. Some photographer came around, took a group picture, and made copies of it and this copy survived and was rediscovered at the reunion in-- in Los Angeles several years ago. This picture now is of me at the age of 12. It's the first picture taken of me in the United States and my name at that time was still Sigfried. This picture is of Fannie and Reggie in 1948, when they first came over from Belgium. That's me on the left standing. Fannie is between-- in the middle-- and on the right is Martin and the one kneeling down or sitting down is Regina, my sister, Regina. This is the earliest picture of them when they arrived in the United States. This picture here is the picture of me taken when I first arrived in Seyre et Nailloux in southern France, when we were on the freight train from Brussels to southern France in 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as you can see, my hair is shaved off. The reason they shaved off all my hair is so we wouldn't get lice. And you noticed the sweater that was knit ad the scarf? That's what we had that winter. You can't see my wooden shoes. But that's me at the age of nine years old in southern France. This is an affidavit in lieu of passport that we all wore when we came to the United States from Marseilles, France. And you can see, this is an enlargement of one that each one of us had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you now can show the bottom part, I'll show you something interesting, a statistic. The. Alright, the photograph that you see on the bottom part of the affidavit is a copy of the one that you've seen before of me in Seyre et Nailloux. The interesting statistic is I was only four feet, two tall, weighed 57 pounds at the time that I was just short of 11 years old. I was quite a small boy. Thank you. This is a picture of the family and now. It was taken on Darren's wedding on May 26, 1996. From left to right it's me, David, Allison, Darren, Debbie, and Daniel. The person that you see now with me is Luba [? Melikova. ?] She is my significant other. We've been going together now for five years. And this was taken also at Darren's wedding. I would like to introduce my youngest son, Darren Findling. I'd like to state this for posterity for Darren's children and grandchildren that Darren was one of the biggest cases I've ever had. And I had to convince his mother to have Darren. And it was probably my greatest challenge as an advocate and as lawyer. And I was able to persuade her not to have-- not to have a miscarriage or an abortion, to have Darren. And I promised her everything, even a full time aide live-in maid, if we could have Darren. And she finally agreed to have Darren. Own And when Darren's time came to arrive, he couldn't wait to arrive. I mean, he-- at three o'clock in the morning we had to rush to the hospital and I was never able to get to South McComb. I had to go to Beaumont Hospital. And before we arrived, Darren already was sticking his head out. And she kept saying-- that his mother kept saying, he's coming out, he's coming out. And I kept saying, push him back in, push him back in. Finally, when we arrived at the hospital, the guard took one look and realized that it was too late, we couldn't take him to the delivery room. So the guard went out and bought cigars and passed them out as being the one responsible for the delivery. And since that time, Darren could never wait to accomplish anything. He was a self-starter, self-motivated. Everything he did was always on time, if not sooner. And perhaps of all my children, he is one that I have to give credit to for getting to where he is without too much parental push. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a great introduction. Thank you. The-- it's-- it's been a-- it's been a great asset to have such a strong person in my life and in our entire family. We're a very, very, very close family. And I don't just mean that we spend our holidays together or spend some time socializing together. We really live each day as a very, very close family. We work together, the three brothers do-- myself and Daniel and David. And our sister, Debbie, who lives in California, although she's far away in distance, she's very, very close to us and we're-- we're an extremely close family. And it all goes back to my father raising us and stressing the importance of a close family because of his lack of a strong family unit while he was a survivor of the Holocaust. And he has impressed upon us how important it is to live each day and to count on your family and to treat each other with great respect. And I think that he-- that's one quality that he brought in as a result of not having a very close family. He-- he stressed this upon us. And it's such a great asset for us, because now we-- we have a tremendous bond between our siblings and our relationship with our father. He's not only-- he's not only my boss here at the Findling Law Firm, but we count on each other in every-- in every regard. As far as a friendship and for important decisions within our lives we always look to our father because we know what a-- what a symbol he is to us and what a strong foundation he is in our family. And it's amazing that someone who survived such a horrible event in his history was able to come out of this with such an incredible amount of strength, courage, and with his feet so firmly planted on the ground and was able to raise four children that are all very successful, that are all very happy, that have-- that have great respect towards each other. And it-- it's really a tribute to an amazing person that he was able to deal with his history but be able to remember that this had occurred in his life, but not let it get in the way of destroying the remaining years that he had. This all occurred at a very, very young age and most people who are affected at a young age have-- have an inability to get over it. And I'm using a kind of a loose word here, but to get over a very traumatic experience in his life. And what he did was he took what he had experienced and learned from it and learned to convey this to his kids and show the importance of-- of a family unit and teaching each other respect towards your parents. And we have tremendous respect for him. If you could-- if-- this tape might be very short, but if you can only see us a day in the life here at this office, if you could see what goes on at this office, it's an amazing thing that his sons can all work together with the father and get along day to day. And it's because he's the-- the foundation that keeps this whole thing together.

speaker: interviewee
text: David, can I interrupt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think at your wedding when I made the speech, it was edited. It's time for me to edit your speech.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could go on and on for hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. But I-- I-- I-- I think what Darren is saying is that if life is any experience and life is an education, what-- what I've learned in life is that your war years, your traumatic experience should not be an albatross around your neck. It should not be something that weighs you down and prevents you from moving forward. It should be and educational experience, it should be part of your history, but you've got to look forward in life in learning from your experiences and making sure that whatever traumatic event you have are not a germ or a-- not a virus that will infect your children and their children and their children's children. You should control and learn to control and put behind you your experiences and allow your children and their children to have a new life based upon the strength of your experiences, not by-- by the traumatic events. And that-- if I've done anything correct in this world, if I've done anything in this life to make sure that the trauma of my past is not an infectious disease that is passed on to my children, I can say that their life is a testament of that. And if I am to give any advice to my children and my grandchildren and to anyone who listens or watches this TV edition of my history, let it be-- let it be something for you to know that there is something that can come out of the tragedy. Something worthwhile can happen that a new future can come into being and the children can benefit from your past and grow strong and pass on that knowledge to generation and generation. If I can just say this in closing, that my history is a history-- is part of the history of the Shoah. It is part of the learning experience of the Jewish people. Always retain your Jewish identity. Never-- [CRYING] --never despair. Be a Jew and be proud of it. [CRYING] Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: is the conclusion of the videotaping session. We used four tapes.
